facts_disclosed: Planning to do CIMA alongside ACA

turns.content: can i ask you something about career strategy in accounting
Of course — what are you thinking about?

so im currently doing my ACA right, ive got an exam in a few days actually. but ive been thinking about what comes after. like once i finish ACA what do i do to actually stand out. and ive been looking into doing CIMA as well

That's an interesting move. ACA and CIMA together is sometimes called being "dual qualified" — it's not super common because most people pick one path and stick with it. ACA is more practice/audit focused while CIMA is management accounting and strategic finance. What's drawing you to CIMA specifically?

exactly that — the strategic side. like ACA is teaching me a lot about audit and compliance but i want to understand the business side too. management accounting, decision making, commercial strategy. if i had both qualifications id be able to go into basically any finance role. audit, advisory, industry, FP&A, whatever. it makes you way more versatile

You're right that the combination opens a lot of doors. Having ACA gives you the technical credibility, and CIMA adds the commercial and strategic lens. Employers in industry especially value dual-qualified candidates because you can speak both languages — you understand the compliance side AND the business performance side.

The big question is timing. When are you thinking of starting CIMA? During your ACA studies or after?

alongside honestly. i know that sounds insane. but theres a pathway where if youve already passed certain ACA papers you get exemptions from some of the CIMA papers. so you dont have to do the full thing from scratch. i think you can knock off like half the CIMA syllabus with ACA exemptions

You're right about the exemptions — CIMA offers a gateway route for ACA holders where you can skip several operational and management level papers. The exact exemptions depend on which ACA papers you've passed, but it can significantly reduce the CIMA workload.

That said, even with exemptions, you'd still be looking at the strategic level papers plus the case study, which is the hardest part of CIMA. And you'd be doing this while finishing your remaining ACA papers AND working full-time. That's a serious workload. How are you finding the ACA workload right now?

loool ok i wont lie the ACA alone is killing me right now. ive literally been cramming for this exam thats in 3 days and im barely keeping it together. BUT i think once this exam is done and busy season is over ill have more capacity. like april through december is much calmer and thats when i could do the CIMA papers

OK so the plan would be: survive March, then use the quieter months to chip away at CIMA while continuing ACA. That's more realistic than trying to do both during busy season.

A realistic timeline might look like:
- **April-June:** Register for CIMA, apply for exemptions, start studying for the first non-exempt paper
- **July-September:** First CIMA exam
- **October-December:** Second CIMA paper, plus any remaining ACA papers
- **Next year:** Strategic case study

Have you looked into whether your firm would sponsor it? Some Big 4 firms will fund additional qualifications.

thats exactly what i was going to ask about. PwC already pays for my ACA so i was wondering if they'd do the same for CIMA. ive heard some teams support it especially if you can make the case that it benefits the firm. like if im in audit but i can also advise clients on management accounting stuff thats a selling point right

Absolutely — that's the pitch. You'd frame it as adding value to the firm, not just personal development. Something like: "Dual qualification allows me to bring a strategic finance perspective to audit clients, which deepens our advisory relationship and positions PwC as a more holistic partner."

The reality is that sponsorship policies vary by office and by team. Here's what I'd do:

1. **Check the internal policy first** — PwC usually has a learning and development portal that outlines what additional qualifications they'll fund
2. **Talk to your counselling manager** — they'll know the approval process and can advocate for you
3. **Find out if anyone else at PwC Manchester has done it** — if there's a precedent, it's much easier to get approved

Even if they don't cover it fully, partial sponsorship or study leave allocation would help.

ok this is bare helpful. i think theres a girl in the tax team who did CIMA alongside her CTA actually so theres definitely precedent for dual quals at the firm. ill find out who she is and ask how she got it approved. having someone whos done it would make the conversation with my manager way easier

That's the smart approach — internal precedent is the strongest argument. If the tax team already approved it for someone, your manager would have a hard time saying no without a good reason.

When you talk to the girl in tax, the key things to ask are:
- Did PwC cover the full cost or partial?
- Did she get additional study leave or did she use her own time?
- How did she manage the workload — any tips for balancing two qualifications?
- Who approved it and what was the process?

That intel will basically give you a blueprint.

yeah exactly. i need to know the process before i go to my manager because i dont want to turn up asking for something without knowing how it works. im going to do some research first and then approach him after my ACA exam when things are calmer. dont want to spring it on him during busy season when hes already stressed loool

Timing is everything with these conversations — bringing it up post-busy season when everyone's calmer and more receptive is the right call. April would be ideal. You'll have just finished an exam, you can lead with the ambition angle, and it's far enough from the next busy season that the workload concern feels manageable.

One more thing to consider: when you pitch it, have a rough study plan ready. Show your manager you've thought about how it fits around work — which papers you'd do when, that you'd focus CIMA study in the quieter months, etc. Managers are much more likely to say yes when they can see you've planned it properly rather than just saying "I want to do another qualification."

thats a really good point. i need to show that ive mapped it out not just that im keen. cos if i just go in and say i want to do CIMA hes gonna be like cool but when and how and i need to have answers. alright im actually really excited about this now. double qualified at 27-28 would look insane on a CV wallah. ACA and CIMA together is proper elite

It would be a seriously strong credential — most people don't even attempt it. At 27-28 with both qualifications, you'd be genuinely competitive for senior roles that typically go to people with years more experience. The versatility it gives you is hard to match.

But first things first — you have an ACA exam in 3 days. Get through that, then let the CIMA planning be your reward for surviving busy season. One thing at a time.

loool yes ok youre right. ACA first. survive march. then world domination. im going back to revision now but thank you for helping me think this through. i feel like i actually have a plan now instead of just a vague idea
Good luck with the exam — you've put the work in, trust it. And when April comes around, you'll have the CIMA plan ready to go. One step at a time. Go smash it.
